碩士學位論文  
學號： M1 1001 010  
 
優化貨架利用率及分配於智動化揀貨系統之研究 
 
Optimizing Pod Utilization and Allocation  
 in Robotic Mobile Fulfillment System  
 
研  究  生： 洪郡澤  
指導教授： 周碩彥  博士 
指導教授： 郭伯勳  博士 
 
中華民國 112 年 07 月





I 
 
摘要 
這項研究的著重在於優化智動化揀貨系統（ RMFS），以實現成本降低。透過應用演
算法來改善裝載過程，並使用多元啟發式來確定每個庫存保管單位（ SKU）的最佳位置。  
我們在這項研究中解決的一個主要問題是貨櫃裝載問題（CLP），這是一個存在了許
多年的 NP-Hard 問題。我們採取的方法有兩個面向，首先，最大化空間利用率，從而減少
插槽的浪費；其次，根據整體訂單數量為每個 SKU 指定策略位置。這兩步方法使我們能
夠計算出有效運作所需的 POD（隨選移動儲存單元）的總數。 
此研究是通過融合現實世界的觀察並調整實證數據來模擬我們的實驗的實際情境。此
實驗的限制也反映了現實世界的約束，包括旋轉方向和不允許在同一插槽中放置不同的
SKU。這些因素使我們的研究更能反映實際倉庫環境所面臨的真實挑戰。 
這項研究的結果顯示，我們的方法在確定滿足訂單要求所需的 POD 數量方面是有效
的，從而幫助倉庫將多餘的 POD 放置的成本降至最低。通過提供一種準確預測所需 POD
數量的方法，我們的研究為改善倉庫效率和降低運營成本的廣泛論述做出了貢獻。我們相
信，這些見解及其相關策略可為希望優化其 RMFS 操作並實現顯著成本降低的企業提供寶
貴的資源。 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
關鍵字：RMFS、揀選訂單分配、貨櫃裝載問題、多元啟發式  

II 
 
ABSTRACT 
 
This research focuses o n optimizing Robotic Mobile Fulfillment Systems (RMFS) for cost 
reduction. The methodology enhances the loading process by applying  advanced algorithms and 
metaheuristics to identify optimal locations for each Stock Keeping Unit (SKU).  
One of the central issues we address in this study is the Container Loading Problem (CLP), 
an NP-Hard problem that has persisted for many years. The approach undertaken here is twofold: 
firstly, to maximize space utilization, thereby reducing the wastage of slots, and second ly, to 
strategically assign locations for each SKU based on the overall order quantity. This two -step 
methodology enables us to calculate the total number of PODs (Portable On Demand storage units) 
required for efficient operations. 
This research was condu cted by incorporating real -world observations and adjusting 
empirical data to simulate a practical context for our experiment. This experiment ’s constraints 
also reflect real -world limitations, including the di

rection of rotation and the restriction agains t 
placing different SKUs in the same slot. These factors make our study more reflective of the 
challenges faced in warehouse environments. 
This research demonstrates  that our method effectively identifies the accurate number of 
PODs required to fulfill the  order requirements, thus helping warehouses  minimize the cost of 
surplus POD placements. By providing a means to a nticipate the required number of PODs 
accurately, our study c

ontributes to the wider discourse on improving warehouse efficiency and 
decreasing operational costs. We believe these insights and the associated strategies presented here 
can be a valuable resource for businesses looking to optimize their RMFS operations and achieve 
significant cost reductions. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Keywords: RMFS, Pick Order Assignment, Container Loading Problem, Metaheuristics

kground 
With the rapid growth of e -commerce and online shopping, there has been an increasing 
demand for efficient and automated order fulfillment systems. According to analysts, the global e-
commerce share of retail sales is predicted to grow this year and reach 20.8%. By 2026, it is 
forecasted to increase by 5.2 percentage points to 24%, indicating significant growth in just six 
ye

ars [1]. Hence, conventional transportation methods are inadequate, and adopting automated and 
efficient means of retrieving and transporting goods is imperative . Achieving automation in the 
warehouse plays a vital role in attaining optimal levels of automation and autonomy, which leads 
to faster, safer, and more efficient day -to-day operations. By lev

eraging automated processes in 
the warehouse, costs are reduced, and delivery times are improved, resul ting in a leaner, more 
scalable, and eco-friendly operation [2].  
Implementing automation in order-picking is possible by utilizing the Robotic Mobile 
Fulfillment System (RMFS), a semi-automated parts-to-picker system commonly employed in e-
commerce warehouses. This system employs an automatic guided vehicle (AG V) to convey 
movable shelves (pods) to the designated picking stations, facilitating the automation process. 
Despite advancements in the implementation of this system, there are several distinct actions 
carri

ed out by different entities at various phases of  the robot routing problem [3], product 
assignment, pod storage allocation [4], layout design [5], and AGV energy consumption [6]. The 
task of executing RMFS is a laborious undertaking that involves numerous intricate subproblems. 
Without proper management, the RMFS cannot run the warehouse smoothly. Therefore, many 
studies have been working on RMFS [7

]. Yang, Jin [8] Conducted a study that analyzed the effect 
of warehouse configuration on the throughput. They analyzed some factors, including lane depth, 
number of aisles, picking stations, and robots. Yang and Hua [9] optimize robot utilization by 
designing a policy for order sequencing and rack scheduling. Keung, Lee [10]  focuses on the 
charging assignment problem. Some recent studies focus on pic k order and pod assignment, 
includ

ing [11, 12].  
Among all problems in RMFS, one problem that also might influence the efficiency of the 
warehouse is the item-to-pod allocation problem. While most of the studies focus on pod allocation 

2 
 
and pod selection based on demand, very limited studies discuss the item -to-pod assignment, 
which also considers the dimension of the items. An optimal item assign ment to the pods might 
reduce the pods required to fulfill the demand. Thus, it can reduce the cost of pod investment, the 
size of the storage area in the warehouse, and the overall warehouse operational cost. Therefore, 
this study aims to improve pod utilization by optimizing the item-to-pod assignment.  
The item -to-pod assignment problem is similar to the three -dimensional (3D) bin -packing 
problem. The 3D bin packing problem, an extension of the one-dimensional and two-dimensional 
problems, is crucial in logistics planning. It is ubiquitous in the real world, encompassing steel, 
textiles, transportation, and manufacturing industries. It aims to increase the utilization rate of 
trucks or

containers to enhance system efficiency. In this study, the 3D bin -packing problem is 
applied in RMFS to reduce the extra costs caused by the need for multiple shelves due to inefficient 
warehouse shelf space utilization.  
The Bin Packing Problem (BPP) is one of the most famous problems in combinatorial 
optimization problems  that manifest in numerous practical applications wi

thin the fields of 
transportation and logistics. According to the improved typology of Wäscher, Haußner [13], BPP 
has been studied since the 1970s [14, 15]. Howev

A similar problem with BPP is the container loading problem (CLP). Both BPP and CLP are 
categorized as NP-hard problems. Until recent years, there has been research proposing the use of 
deep learning to solve such problems. However, heuristic algo rithms remain the most favored by 
many. Hajlaoui and Jaoua [16] present a new Reinforcement Learning model for container loading. 
It performs well and shows that l ower training variability yields better results  than high training 
variabil

ity. Kurpel and Scarpin [17] offer new, effective solutions for container loading problems, 
considering conditions like box orientation, load stability, and separation of boxes. Zhang, Gu [18] 
introduces a new hybrid heuristic algorithm to address the container loading problem efficiently . 
Gajda, Trivella [19]  introduces a new heuristic algorithm to solve a comp lex logistics problem, 
balancing cargo value and multiple constraints.  
A pod in RMFS consists of several slots.

The slots can have the same or different sizes. 
Maximizing the number of items stored in a slot is similar to the 3D bin packing problem. This 
study proposes a heuristics algorithm to design the item’s arrangement in a slot to optimize the 
slot’s utilization. Furthermore, because there are many different slots and pods, the next problem 
is which item should be stored in which slot in which

This problem is a combinatorial problem. 

3 
 
Therefore, this study applies a metaheuristics approach to solve this problem. Regarding algorithm 
selection, we adopted GA and PSO, which have been widely used [20-23], as our methodologies. 
1.2 Objectives 
This study aims to optimize pod utilization by maximizing the number of items stored in each 
slot and finding the best item -slot-pod

arrangement. This study combines heuristics and 
metaheuristics algorithms to find the optimal solution. T his study applies two metaheuristics 
algorithms: particle swarm optimization (PSO) and genetic

algorithm (GA).  
1.3 Relevance of Past Solutions to Current CLP Challenges 
1. The complexity of the Problem: CLP is an NP-hard problem, meaning there is no known 
polynomial-time solution. This  condition makes finding the optimal solution extremely 
challenging for large-scale problems. 
2.

Limitations of Heuristic Approaches: Many studies employ heuristic methods to solve 
CLP, but these methods usually can only find approximate solutions rather than the 
optimal ones. Moreover, the effectiveness of these methods might vary depending on the 
specifics of the problem. 
3.

Lack of Experimental Data: Many studies might lack experimental data to validate their 
methods. This  lack of data  could be due to difficulty acquiring real -world packing 
problem data or because conducting experiments requires significant time and resources. 
1.4 Scope and Limitations 
The following presents a comprehensive and detailed overview of both the scope and 
limitations of the present research: 
• The data used in this study has been derived by modifying actual cases. 
• The loading pattern adopted in this research considers overlapping and over stepping of 
SKUs. 
• A pod can accommodate multiple slots, but each slot can hold only one SKU. 
• This study has considered six distinct approaches to the placement of SKUs. 
• This study permits stacking upwards and pushing backward during the loading process. 
• This study will utilize 5,729 SKUs, a considerably large number.

4 
 
1.5 Organizations of Thesis 
This section primarily delineates the context and objectives of our study, alongside 
acknowledging the limitations and scope of our research. The thesis’s subsequent sections are 
structured as follows. Section 2 reviews the existing literature concerning t he 3D -BPP and 
scrutinizes the variances between this study and prior research. Section 3 of the thesis expounds 
on the research methodology and elaborates on the scope

of the study. The specific aspects of the 
solution approaches will provide (loading patt ern and heuristics) in Section 4. Lastly, the paper 
concludes and elucidates future research direct

ions in Section 5.   
  

5 
 
CHAPTER 2  
LITERATURE REVIEW 
 
2.1 Robotic Mobile Fulfillment System 
In numerous warehouses, products or items are traditionally stacked on shelves i n an 
organized manner to facilitate retrieval, and personnel, often referred to as pickers, actively pick 
and pack these items according to specific customer orders. In situations with an intense flux of 
goods moving in and out of the warehouse, which is common in large-scale operations, Automated 
Storage/Retrieval Systems (AS/RS) are widely employed to stream

line the process and enhance 
efficiency. However, this conventional warehouse automation method has limitations, including a 
rigid structure that make s altering inventory locations cumbersome, relatively high initial setup 
costs, and manual intervention in reallocating resources. 
To combat these inherent challenges and to revolutionize the warehousing industry, Robotic 
Mobile Fulfillment Systems (RMFS) have been introduced as an innovative solution. RMFS, 
characterized by its integration of cutting-edge robotics, is tailor-made for the unique demands of 
e-commerce warehouses. At the core of the RMFS is an automated parts -to-picker system.

This 
system employs autonomous robots that tirelessly transport movable shelves, aptly termed “pods,” 
to and fro between storage areas and workstations.  This system’s ingenuity lies in eliminating  
walking time for pickers, which is achieved by having the pods brought dir ectly to them. This  
process dramatically increases the picking rate, thereby enhancing overall productivity. 
Within the workstations, there is a hive of activity where inventory is methodically placed 
onto the pods or diligently retrieved from them based o n the requirements of the order at hand. 
Nevertheless, implementing RMFS has complexities and challenges  [24]. Notably, logistical 
issues include  the strategic assignment of customer orders to workstations and the efficient 
allocation of pods to sto

rage locations  [25]. These decision-making processes are not trivial and 
require intricate trade-offs to optimize the system. For instance, it might be logical to prioritize an 
order nearing its deadline by assigning it to a workstation. However,  synergizing it with another  
order that shares a common aisle with the orders assigned to that workstation could lead to more 
efficient picking strategies. This condition might entail more picks per pod, reducing the trips each 
pod needs to make, culminating in enhanced efficiency an d resource optimization. The 
implementation of RMFS, therefore, necessitates careful planning and intelligent decision-making 

6

to harness its potential fully [26]. 
Based on past research [11, 27-29], there is a plethora of literature concerning the placement 
of SKUs in pods. Some studies have considered factors such as the association of SKU locations, 
the correlation in picking, batch processi

ng, and the distance to move to other sites. Additionally, 
heuristic algorithms have been proposed to minimize inventory costs as much as possible.  This 
study integrates the CLP with this issue and employs heuristic algorithms

to enhance the efficiency 
of solving the problem. 
2.2 Container loading problem 
The CLP is a geometric assignment challenge that entails loading a subset of rectangular 
items, often denoted as SKU (Stock Keeping Units), into a fixed-dimension rectangular container, 
such as pods. The aim is to maximize the volume of the packed boxes. The packing process has to 
satisfy an optimized objective function under two primary geometric feasibility prerequisites: 
• Every small item must be completely contained within the large object, and 
• There should be no overlapping among the smaller units.  
CLP are diverse, with various properties constituting their characteristics. They are 
traditionally categorized into two groups, as outlined by Wäscher and Haußner [13

]. In the input 
minimization problem, the first category  assumes sufficient storage space for all boxes. In such 
cases, the number of containers is usually non -restrictive, with the primary objective being to 
minimize the number of containers required to accommodate all boxes. Conversely, the second 
group, the output maximization problem, operates under limited container space, which is 
insufficient for storing all boxes. The aim here is to select a subset of boxes to maximize the 
volume or value

of the load. 
The classifications of cutting and packing problems proposed by Dyckhoff [30] and Wäscher, 
Haußner [13]  offer solid foundations for investigations in this field. According to Wäscher, 
Haußner [13], these problems can be grouped based on dimensionality, the variety of large and 
small items

, and the objective. Within the scope of this paper, the focus is primarily on 3D 
rectangular items, with discussions on both single-bin and multi-bin packing problems. 
2.2.1 Single bin packing problem 
The single-bin packing problem is a classic cutting and packing problem, where the objective 
is to place a set of items into the minimum number of

bins of fixed dimensions. Each item has a 

7 
 
certain volume or weight, while each bin has a maximum capacity. The challenge lies in allocating 
the items into the b

ins to maximize space utilization. Most of the literature concerning packing 
problems focuses on this type of problem, and there are various adaptations based on different 
problem descriptions, including the development of corresponding mathematical models and 
algorithms (Phongmoo, Leksakul [31] and Araya, Guerrero [32])  
2.2.2 Multiple bin packing problem 
The multi-bin packing problem extends the single-bin packing problem, where items can be 
divided into multiple categories or sets. Items from each set need to be packed into the same bin, 
and each bin can only contain items from a single set. Compared to the single -bin scenario, the 
multi-bin problem has received less attention. The objective is to satisfy the item s’ requirements 
with the minimum number of containers. In past literature,  Alonso and Alvarez-Valdes [33]  
addressed this issue by minimizing the number of trucks used  and solving it through an integer 
linear model while complying with certain constraints. 
Ideally, we want to use the fewest shelves and have each slot filled as completely as possible. 
However, these two objectives may conflict in practice and must be balanced appropriately. 
2.3 Common Solutions in the Past 
The CLP is a complex combinatorial problem. R esearch on it has been steadily increasing 
over the past dec

ades. In most studies, heuristic or metaheuristic methods are applied to solve this 
problem, although exact algorithms are also available. Heuristic methods provide solutions quickly 
and are effec tive for problems demanding real -time decisions or when computational resources 
are limited. The simplicity of heuristics contributes to their accessibility and ease of understanding. 
However, these methods, designed specifically for a problem, may not alw ays find the optimal 
solution, and their performance can significantly vary depending on the problem instance. Huang 
and Hwang [34] propose a heuristic algorithm for resolving the three-dimensional single-container 
loading problem through optimal plac

ement.  Owing to the NP -hard nature of the problem, it is 
framed as a zero -one mixed integer linear programming model. The suggested heuristic has 
demonstrated superior efficiency to the cutting-edge mixed integer linear program and pre-existing 
heuristic approaches. Gajda, Trivella [19]  proposed Randomized Constructive Heuristic (RCH) 
algorithm quickly generates superior packing solution s and effectively addresses a logistics

company’s CPL. 

8 
 
On the other hand, exact algorithms guarantee solutions ’ optimality, given enough 
computational time. They provide a reliable and precise way to benchmark the quality of heuristic 
and

metaheuristic solutions. However, their main limitation is computational inefficiency  for 
large-scale problems due to their exhaustive nature, making them impractical for real -time 
decision-making or problems with large dimensions. Martello and Pisinger [35] offered an exact 
branch-and-bound method to find optimal solutions for small -scale instances of the three -
dimensiona

l bin packing problem (3D-BPP). Although exact algorithms ensure optimal solutions, 
their applicability is constrained for cases involving many boxes. 
Metaheuristics, being more general, offer flexibility in application across various problem 
domains. They tend to explore a broader search space and can often provide good-quality solutions, 
even for complex problems. However, they require careful tuning of parameters for each problem, 
and there is no guarantee of finding an optimal solution. Kurpel and Scarpin [17]  introduce 
mathematical models for various container loading problems, evaluate box placement techniques, 
and propose new methods to set  boundaries for input minimization issues. It uses well -known 
datasets for computational experiments, oft

en achieving new or improved optimal solutions. 
Metaheuristics, adaptable to various problems, have also been employed in tackling the 
Container Loadin g Problem (CLP), in contrast to heuristics, which are tailored for specific 
problems. Adapting these metaheuristics necessitates modifications to accommodate the 
representation of CLP solutions. The roster of employed metaheuristics encompasses Genetic 
Algorithms (GA) [20-23], PSO, Artificial Bee Colony Optimization  [36],

Tabu Search [37], and 
Simulated Annealing, among others. 
This study addresses a Single Large Object Placement Problem (SLOPP), striving to place 
small items (SKUs) into containers (Pods). Given various Pod sizes, this study utilizes a 
metaheuristic app

roach to identify the most appropriate placement for each SKU. Ultimately, the 
objective is to fulfill the same amount of order requirements with the minimum number of shelves. 
2.4 Metaheuristics 
Metaheuristics have found significant applicability in Container Loading Problems (CLPs) 
due to their flexibility, adaptability, and effectiveness in handling complex combinatorial 
optimization i ssues. As CLPs are NP -hard, signifying inherent computational complexity 
intensifying with problem size, f

This condition paves the way for heuristic and metaheuristic methods. With their high 

9 
 
adaptability, metaheuristics  can accommodate various optimization problems, includin

g CLPs. 
Their stochastic processes help avoid premature convergence and encourage a broader solution to 
space exploration . Balancing between exploring new solution a reas and intensively searching 
promising ones, they efficiently generate near -optimal solutions within reasonable timeframes. 
Although they do not guarantee an optimal solution, their delivery of high-quality solutions makes 
them crucial in efficientl

y solving CLPs. 
This study utilizes the GA and PSO algorithms  for comparison. Figure 2.1 shows the 
flowcharts of the PSO and GA algorithms. The choice of these two algorithms is predicated on 
their widespread application in CLP. This study aims to use heuristic algorithms to assign SKUs 
to the most appropriate slots, thereby minimizing the ultimate use of Pods. Table 2.1 presents the 
past literature on the application of algorithms to the Container Loading Problem (CLP).

CHAPTER 3  
METHODOLOGY 
 
This section discusses the research methodology of this study. In the first step, th

is study 
represents the problem as a mathematical model to get a better problem definition. Furthermore, 
as the problem can be divided into two main problems, the item-to-slot arrangement, and SKU-to-
pod assignment problems, this study solves each problem with different approaches. The item-to-
slot arrangement problem is how to arrange the items into a slot so that the number of items stored 
in a slot can be maximized. This problem is simil ar to a 3D CLP. This study solves this problem 
with a heuristics approach. Figure 3.1 represents the flowchart of the execution process in this 
st

udy. 
 Besides optimizing the item’s arrangement in a slot, finding the best SKU-to-pod assignment 
is also crucial to minimize the number of pods. As this problem is a combinatorial problem, this 
study utilizes genetic algorithms and particle swarm optimization algorithms to find the best SKU-
to-pod a

ssignment. 
  
Figure 3.1 Research Flowchart 


12 
 
3.1 Dimensionality Reduction with DBSCAN Clustering 
DBSCAN stands for Density-Based Spatial Clustering of Applications with Noise [38]. It is 
particularly valued for its capability to detect clusters of arbitrary shapes, based on the density of 
data points, without specifying the numbe

r of clusters beforehand. 
The algorithm employs two parameters, eps representing the maximum distance between two 
data points to be considered neighbors and min_samples, indicating the minimum number of points 
required to form a dense region. DBSCAN classifies points as core, border, or noise, with core 
points forming the clusters [38]. 
DBSCAN’s ability to identify clusters of different shapes and handle noise efficiently has led 
to its widespread application in diverse fields like astronomy2 and bioinformatics [39]. However, 
the choice of parameters can significantly impact the results, and DBS CAN may struggle with 
clusters of varying densities. 
This study will use this method to categorize thousands of SKUs (Stock Keeping Units)  to 
reduce operational complexity. Moreover, it ems with large volumes are unlikely to  be placed in 
the same pod. We have considered this issue and aim to distinguish SKUs through clustering. 
3.2 Mathematical Formulation for the CLP 
The item-to-pod arrangement or assignment can be represented as an extension of 3D C

LP. 
This study defines this problem’s main objective as minimizing the number of pods that serve all 
demands. In order to minimize the number of pods, the utilization of each slot in each pod should 
be maximized. Therefore, the first problem represented in the mathematical model is the items’ 
arrangement in a slot to maximize the slots’ utilization.  
This study adopts a 3D cartesian coordinate system, with the left -bottom-back vertex as the 
origin point, as shown in Figure 3.2. The items are arranged orthogonally within the container. 
In this section, we reference previous literature concerning the mathematical models of 3D -
CLP, from which we have collated the relevant mathematical expressions for the problem 
presented in this study.

This study employs a Genetic Algorithm (GA) to solve the SKU-to-pod assignment

The solution of SKU-to-pod assignment is the allocation of each SKU to available slots in all pods. 
Therefore, this study represents a set of pods with several slots as  a chromosome. Each pod 


18 
 
represents a gene. Inside each gene, there are slots. For example, the chromosome would contain 
ten genes if a warehouse had ten pods, each with nine slots, as shown in Figure 3.6. 
Because two main decisions should be encountered in the chromosome, each gene comprises 
two parts. The first part is the activation part, which informs whether the warehouse utilizes the 
correspondence pod. The value of the first part is a binary number.

The active pod has a value of 
1, while the inactive pod has a value of 0. The second part represents a set of slots in the pod. The 
value is a discrete number representing the SKUs’ IDs. This scheme is explained further in Figure

3.5 Application of Particle Swarm Optimization in CLP 
The second algorithm employed in this study is the PSO algorithm. While the GA algorithm 
represents the SKU-to-Pod assignment as a chromosome, PSO represents it as a particle with the 
same configuration as the chromosome.  Drawing parallels to the Genetic Al gorithm (GA) 
principles, each particle undergoes updates influenced by the particle ’s optimal historical 
movement and the optimal movement observed w

ithin the entire swarm. Let P be a set of particles,

icle equates to the solution. With each iteration, particles are updated continuously 
according to Equations ( 3-18 ) and ( 3-19 ). The particles traverse the search space through the

We evaluate whether the solution adheres t

o the set conditions upon executing the

within the POD. Consequently, if each SKU fulfills

the stipulated quantity, that solution is deemed 
feasible. If it does not, we identify the SKUs that have been excessively placed and not adequately 
placed and make substitutions until the condition is met. However, if there are no excessively 
placed SKUs, we will continue to add an empty POD and

keep placing the unsatisfied SKUs until

4.1 Data description  
Our dataset comprises 5,729 distinct SKUs and three different types of PODs. Each POD has

one of the POD types to address our research objectives. We further divided the SKU dataset into 
seven categories based on the degree of variation and quantity. These are categorized into strong, 
medium, and weak heterogeneity and differences in demand following the Poisson distribution.

5.1 Conclusion 
In the ever-changing landscape of consumer behavior, e -commerce entities find themselves 
in a state of perpetual evolution, constantly

seeking advancements and improvements. Central to 
their mission is a dual objective - curbing process and demand costs while satisfying customer 
needs. A crucial element of this mission involves a logistical challenge: determining the optimal 
quantity of pods required within the constraints of limited warehouse space. 
Therefore, to tackle this challenge, various strategies must be considered: effective placement 
of stock in slots, the appropriate designation of location for each Stock Keeping Unit (SKU), and 
many more. Our study em braces the reality of these operational c

omplexities and contributes a 
proposed solution. By leveraging a novel 3D -CLP algorithm and harnessing the power of 
metaheuristics, we have devised a solution tailored to these intricate real-world problems. 
In the context of this study, it is noteworthy that our focus is on the 3D CLP. Attempting to 
express the complexities of this problem through a conventional mathematical model would prove 
overly complex and demand a significant time investment. To bypass these h urdles, we adopt a 
heuristic appr

oach. Our strategy zeroes in on SKU placement in slots, aiming for optimal space 
utilization. 
Building on this, we employ a sinlge objective heuristic approach to discern the ideal 
placement for each SKU. We aim to reduce the number of pods required to meet our operational

The experimental results underscore the considerable impact of parameter changes. Our 
experiment incorporated six distinct scenarios, characterized by different levels of heterogeneity - 
strong, medium, and weak - and differences in quantities, specifically low and high dema

nd. The 
best average pod  needed in these scenarios was quantified as 10.90, 30.10, 13.10, 39.40, 19.00, 
and 103.30, respectively, which all the best average results obtained by using PSO. 
An interesting pattern emerged when comparing the performance of GA and PSO in these 
scenarios. Despite the longer time commitment necessitated by PSO, it consistently yielded 
superior results. This result reinforces the merit of investing mo re time upfront to derive more 
efficient and optimal outcomes. 

36 
 
5.2 Future Research 
In the intricate realm of Robotic Mobile Fulfillment Systems (RMFS), the dynamics of every 
operation hold paramount significance. Each action, whether latent or swift, can send 
reverberations throughout the system, instigating a chain reaction of effects o n the subsequent 
tasks. In such a tightly -knit environment, even a slight deviation in one area can echo across the 
entire ecosystem, emphasizing the delicate interplay of components. A holistic approach becomes 
essential in a world where isolated solutions often fall short. 
Our current study is grounded in real-world scenarios, drawing heavily from conditio

ns typical 
to the Picking area. However, the breadth and depth of the RMFS world mean that our model, 
though robust, has ample room for expansion and fu rther fine -tuning. The more nuances we 
incorporate, the closer our mo

Stock allocation within the PODs is far from a straightforward task. Fundamental 
considerations, such as the weight-bearing capacity of each POD, must be juxtaposed against the 
collective endurance of the RMFS. It is a balancing act, ensuring we utilize the system to its fullest 
potential without overburdening it. Similarly, the strategic placement of stock is not just about 
space optimization but also about understanding the interrelationships between different Stock 
Keeping Units (SKUs). By analyzing buying patterns and SKU compatibility, we can create a 
layout where frequently co-purchased items are in proximity, streamlining retrieval processes and 
enhancing operational efficiency. 
Central to our approach is the multi-objective heuristic algorithms, which, in their current form, 
represent just the tip of the iceberg. The possibilities are vast: from refining these algorithms to 
overhauling their architecture, every tweak can lead to substantial gains in comp

utational speed. 
In doing so, we can pave the way for more agile, real-time solutions that resonate even more with 
the challenges of real-world RMFS. These enhancements can enrich the proposed model, bringing 
it closer to addressing the multifaceted challenges encountered in real-world RMFS.
